The Matrix benefits from being impeccably crafted and brought to life from a deep place of creative joy. It also benefits from perfect timing with its release year at the cusp of the digital revolution. It exists on so many levels of art and entertainment. It is a fanciful action pact and visually original summer fun movie as its base. When a film is this good we forget that that should be enough to judge it as a masterpiece of pop corn entertainment. However, The Matrix has become a cultural touch stone and starting point for conversations about believe, technologies roll in human lives and what makes a person, “real”.

The Matrix is a movie I’ve seen around eight or nine times, and honestly, it gets better every time. The visual style and aesthetic still hold up, the main characters feel mysterious and cool, and the action remains fun and exciting. I first watched it when I was about nine years old, and now at 32 I rewatched it with my wife. Back then I was more into the action; now I appreciate the philosophical themes much more. It doesn’t go extremely deep, but it definitely makes you think. This was my wife’s first time watching it. She doesn’t watch many movies and didn’t grow up with the same pop culture references, so at first she was confused, but as the movie progressed everything clicked and the story made sense. The dialogue is very dry and sometimes unintentionally funny, but that stiffness actually fits the cold, controlled tone of the movie. The special effects do show their age, but considering the film is over 20 years old, it’s still very enjoyable. A movie that grows with you and is always worth revisiting.

27 years later on the big IMAX screen with big sound this film still ranks as one of my all time favourites. The story so engrossing, the special effects remain remarkable, Keanu Reeves and Hugo Weaving deliver truly marvellous performances. Some references made in the film to the rise of AI chillingly resonate today. The sellout audience’s reaction throughout this re-release remind me how cinema experiences can also lift the enjoyment factor by many notches.

There was a stretch of time where every day after work I went and watched this film. I'd never seen anything like it and it hit me differently than any other movie had before. I was a younger man that had gotten out of the Army recently and life didn't really seem real. I was struggling financially and going through a divorce. So, my circumstances at the time had a lot to do with how I perceived this film. So there it is; the reason for my 5 star review.

Saying that 'The Matrix' is a dystopia is to prove it right. More than a sci-fi revolution, the film marked a turning point in cinema, redefining how digital and practical effects could coexist while introducing new ways to choreograph action, manipulate time, and relate the body to space. Beneath its iconic imagery, 'The Matrix' questions the very notion of reality, echoing Cartesian philosophy: we believe what we perceive because we have no alternative. The film challenges faith in the real, explores destiny versus free will, and turns freedom into an open question rather than a certainty. It also reflects on hope and humanity’s relationship with machines—an idea even more unsettling today. Decades later, its visual language remains unmatched, and its impact undeniable. Iconic, influential, and unforgettable, 'The Matrix' endures not just for what it says, but for how it says it.

I am not an action movie type person, but I still loved The Matrix because it is so intelligent, intriguing, and full of deeper themes to dive into and ponder. The plot line is airtight and I found myself getting more and more engaged and excited as the movie went on. Even though there is violence, it wasn't gratuitous and made sense within the context of the story line. And some of the special effects were so unique and unlike anything I have seen before. I also like that there is some romance sprinkled in because it helps to humanize the characters. After 25 years, I understand the hype.

One of the most iconic movies of my generation For a movie made in the late ’90s, The Matrix has absolutely incredible production value. The visual effects and animation techniques were way ahead of their time — the slow-motion “bullet time” shots and slick fight choreography still hold up amazingly well today. It’s one of those films that completely changed the game for action and sci-fi movies. From start to finish, The Matrix keeps you on the edge of your seat. The story is mind-bending, the pacing is perfect, and the atmosphere pulls you deep into its futuristic world. It’s one of those rare movies that makes you think while still delivering nonstop entertainment. Overall, I’d give The Matrix a solid 4 stars. It’s stylish, thought-provoking, and packed with action — an iconic film that still feels fresh decades later.

Even decades later, The Matrix (1999) remains a landmark in science fiction cinema. Blending groundbreaking visuals like the now-legendary Bullet Time with a thought-provoking exploration of reality, it offered audiences both spectacle and substance. Neo’s journey into a world where the only limits are mental redefined action filmmaking, with wire-fu combat and practical effects giving the movie a timeless quality that CGI-heavy productions of the era often lack. While the Wachowskis’ dialogue occasionally stumbles, the film’s mix of philosophy, suspense, and unforgettable set pieces still captivates. A genre classic that continues to inspire, even if its once-shocking message no longer hits quite as hard.
